title: London By William Blake | Summary and line by line explanation | analysis
description:
Few poems capture urban misery with the cold precision of a surgeon's scalpel quite like William Blake's "London," written in 1794 as part of *Songs of Experience* — where every "charter'd street" and "mind-forg'd manacle" indicts an entire social order. For BA English Honours students at DU, SOL, and IGNOU, this poem is essential because Blake doesn't just describe poverty and oppression — he locates their source in human-made systems of power, making it dangerously radical even today. If you've ever wondered why a short 16-line poem still appears on every major English literature syllabus, this explanation in Hindi will show you exactly why.
